title: Expressing support for the designation of the week of May 9 through May 18, 2025, as "National American Birding Week".
plain_summary: This bill supports making May 9 through May 18, 2025 a special week called National American Birding Week. The bill says that bird watching is good for the economy and helps people learn about birds. It also recognizes that many birds that travel long distances are in danger and need protection. The bill encourages people to join bird watching activities during this week to help support efforts to save birds.
last_action_text: Sponsor introductory remarks on measure. (CR H1926)

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution shows support for naming the week of May nine through May eighteen, two thousand twenty five, as National American Birding Week.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: About three and a half billion songbirds, hunting birds, shore birds, and waterfowl travel north through the United States every spring. These birds represent over three hundred fifty different types of birds. They fly from their winter homes in the southern United States, Mexico, and tropical areas to their breeding areas up north. Many of these traveling bird groups are getting smaller in number. This happens because they lose places to live, other animals hunt them, and they crash into buildings, power lines, and cars. About ninety six million Americans watch birds as a hobby. These bird watchers spend almost one hundred seven billion dollars every year on their bird watching activities. This spending creates one million four hundred thousand jobs and brings in billions of dollars in worker pay and tax money for federal and state governments. There are yearly bird watching events like the Biggest Week in American Birding. This event is run by the Black Swamp Bird Observatory in Oak Harbor, Ohio. They work together with the Ottawa National Wildlife Refuge, the Magee Marsh State Wildlife Area, and many public, nonprofit, and private partners. These events use government, nonprofit, and private money to help protect traveling bird populations and help local economies grow. They do this through research, teaching, and community outreach.

sections.title: House of Representatives Statements
sections.text: The House of Representatives supports naming this week as National American Birding Week. The House recognizes that bird watching is a hobby that brings in billions of dollars in economic benefits every year. The House understands that traveling bird populations face many natural and human caused dangers that threaten their survival. The House applauds the working together of federal, state, and local governments, conservation organizations, and businesses to promote protecting traveling birds and economic development through bird watching. The House encourages bird watchers and the general public to attend events such as the Biggest Week in American Birding to learn about bird migration and to support efforts by governments, conservation organizations, and businesses to preserve traveling bird populations.
